segments.text:
Popular immunological techniques used in research
Western blots
Immunofluorescence
Immunoprecipitation
ELISA
Fluorescent Activated Cell Sorting (FACS)

Why would anyone want to make antibodies?
If you are trying to understand the function of a gene by deleting it, you could prove the gene was gone if your protein disappeared in the mutant.
If you are trying to understand the function of a gene, determining its location in a tissue, or even better, its sub-cellular distribution is very important.
If you want to determine what proteins interact with a protein you are interested in, antibodies can be used to precipitate your protein and other proteins that stick to your protein of interest.
If you are testing for the presence of a bacteria or virus in a sample, antibodies are very sensitive detectors.
There are many antibodies already for sale…….

Generation of Primary Antibody
One can use:
Purified protein
-not used very often, difficult to get high expression, and protein that is pure enough
Cloned gene-expressed protein
-paste cDNA into expression vector with a tag, turn on protein in bacteria.  Purify highly expressed protein by using tag.
Predict peptide from gene sequence
-easy, but expensive

Example of Antigen preparation from cDNA
Protein gel of bacterial
cDNA fusion constructs                                    expressed products
Tag allows purification of protein from columns

Custom Antibody Production
-Easy,  a little pricey
guaranteed

Primary antibodies (polyclonal) generally generated in either mouse or rabbits
Inject protein into animal, wait, boost, draw blood
Spin down cells, use plasma

Protein extraction
Proteins are typically size-fractionated on Polyacrylamide gels

Running gels
Size standards                Pre-made gels                    Load and run

Example of a typical gel showing Total protein stained with Coomassie Blue
Note: these cells make thousands of proteins, how to tell which is which?
There are multiple species at about every position.

-Transfer of proteins to solid support (immobilon)
-Add Primary antibody and blocker
-Incubate overnight
-Wash off primary antibody
-Add secondary antibody
-Incubate 1 hour
-Wash off secondary antibody

Primary antibody
-finds protein of interest with antibody generated as above
Secondary antibody
-finds primary antibody. Uses an antibody with a tag.  An example of a secondary is goat-anti-rabbit conjugated with fluorescein

Cheap, versatile
Expensive, difficult
Secondary Antibodies are made in large animals, purifed and conjugated with enzymes or fluorescent molecules
Examples of secondary antibodies you can buy:
Goat-anti-mouse-Alkaline Phosphatase (AP)
Goat-anti-rabbit horseradish peroxidase (HRP)
Goat-anti-rabbit-fluorescein
Goat-anti-rabbit-immunogold
Use of Primary and Secondary Antibodies

Luminol gets oxidized by Horse Radish Peroxidase (HRP)
Luminol                                                                        light
HRP is conjugated to secondary antibody

Cover blot with X-ray film in the dark, let it expose to light
Develop X-ray film in the darkroom
Summary of Western blots

Example of Western Blots
This confirms that the deletion mutant, rtp1 does not have the RTP protein

Note about cross-reactivity
Its common.  Typically only publish small band where protein is known to be.
Re-introduce gene into the deletion mutant, and protein comes back
Do other mutants affect RTP levels?

Immunoprecipitation (IP)/Co-immunoprecipitation (Co-IP)
-antibody can be used to bind, then precipitate a protein of interest, along with other proteins that stick to it.
-then you can use other antibodies to identify these other proteins that stick to yours

Example of  Immunoprecipitation and Co-immunoprecipitation
Input is total protein before precipitation
a-Retinophilin antibody co-IPs p174 (ninaC myosin)
a-ninaC            a-ninaC                                        a RTP (Retinophilin)
a-p174antibody co-IPs retinophilin

Conjugation scheme for the Plus Activated Peroxidase. In this diagram, the activated HRP is represented by the blue (P) and the antibody by the yellow (P). Antibodies (IgG) are about three times the size of HRP and contain numerous primary amines. The default kit procedure typically results in 1 to 3 HRP molecules per IgG molecule.
